answer:
**The central image in Allama Iqbal's "Ghurra-e-" (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 8) is the first crescent moon of Shawwal (the Eid crescent, *ghurra-e-Shawwal*), personified as a radiant witness and mirror reflecting the glorious past and lamentable present of the Muslim ummah.**[^1]

This crescent is invoked from the title and opening lines—"The First Crescent of Shawwal, or The Eid Crescent" and "O, crescent of Shawwal, light to the fasting eye"—as a symbol of renewal, joy after Ramadan, and ancestral valor (e.g., under battle flags stained with enemy blood).[^2][^3] The poem repeatedly urges it to "behold" (*dekho*) contemporary Muslim disunity, poverty, sectarianism, sycophancy, and geopolitical woes (e.g., the Caliphate's fall), contrasting its freedom and silver glow with the community's "fallen state."[^4] It closes by advising the moon to silently observe like a mirror, lost in echoes of past glory.[^5]

The wiki card confirms this via TextRank's top key passage (the title) and distinctive trigrams like "first crescent shawwal." The poem's structure—19 verse paragraphs of apostrophe and *dekho* refrains—centers the crescent as the unifying symbol of hope amid decline.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 8: "Ghurra-e-" ([full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/08-ghurra-e/translation/)).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-13a49b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/08-ghurra-e/translation/#p-13a49b).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-0d32d9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/08-ghurra-e/translation/#p-0d32d9).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-04fff7](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/08-ghurra-e/translation/#p-04fff7).
[^5]: Ibid., [p-2e9f06](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/08-ghurra-e/translation/#p-2e9f06).
